fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A displayed category is defined to be a univalent displayed precategory.
A displayed precategory C over a precategory B, written B\vdash C, is specified by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each b\in B, a type \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} b of displayed objects over b that we shall write b.c\in C;
for each b_0.c_0, b_1.c_1\in C and i\colon b_0\to b_1, a set C _{i} \mathopen {}\left \lparen b_0.c_0,b_1.c_1\right \rparen \mathclose {} of displayed arrows over i;
for each b.c\in C a displayed identity arrow 1_{b}.1_{c}\colon b.c\to b.c;

for each i.f\colon b_0.c_0\to b_1.c_1 and j.g\colon b_1.c_1\to b_2.c_2, a displayed composite arrow \mathopen {}\left \lparen j\circ i\right \rparen \mathclose {} \circ \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1;
such that composition is associative and unital with respect to displayed identity arrows.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given two displayed objects b.c,b.c' in a displayed precategory B\vdash C, we shall refer to displayed arrows of the form 1_{b}.f\colon b.c\to b.c' as vertical.

Let B\vdash C be a displayed precategory. We define the fiber of C over b\in B to be the precategory b.C whose objects are given by displayed objects b.c\in C and whose arrows b.c_0\to b.c_1 are given by vertical displayed arrows 1_{b}.f\colon b.c_0\to b.c_1, with identities and compositions inherited from C.
A displayed precategory B\vdash C is called univalent when each of its fiber precategories b.C is univalent.
A displayed category is defined to be a univalent displayed precategory.

The total (pre)category B.C of a displayed precategory B\vdash C is defined so that \mathopen {}\left \lvert B.C\right \rvert \mathclose {} is the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b\in B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b\in B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b\in B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b\in B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} b of displayed objects of C and B.C \mathopen {}\left \lparen b.c,b'.c'\right \rparen \mathclose {} is the type of displayed morphisms i.f\colon b.c\to b'.c' in C, with identities and compositions inherited from C.
Given a displayed precategory B\vdash C, we may define a functor \pi _{B\vdash C}\colon B.C\to B called the display functor, sending b.c \in B.C to b and i.f\colon b.c\to b'.c' to i\colon b\to b'.

fr:title.text: Conditions for the total precategory to be univalent

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Conditions for the total precategory to be univalent

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let B\vdash C be a precategory displayed over a precategory B. If B is univalent and B\vdash C is univalent, then the total precategory B.C is univalent.

fr:title.text: Univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Give an example of a displayed category B\vdash C over a precategory B such that the total precategory B.C is univalent but B is nonetheless not univalent.
Let B\vdash C,D be two displayed precategories. A vertical displayed functor from C to D over B, written B.F \colon C\to D, is given by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each displayed object b.c\in C, a displayed object b.Fc \in D;
for each displayed arrow i.f\colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1 in C, a displayed arrow i.Ff\colon b_0.Fc_0\to b_1.Fc_1 in D;
such the assignment of displayed arrows preserves displayed identities and displayed composites.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let B.F, B.G\colon C\to D be two vertical displayed functors between two displayed precategories B\vdash C,D. A vertical displayed natural transformation from B.F to B.G over B, written B.\alpha \colon B.F\to B.G, is given by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each displayed object b.c\in C, a vertical arrow b.\alpha _c\colon b.Fc\to b.Gc;

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The material on displayed categories is drawn from Ahrens and Lumsdaine, although we have imposed our own terminological and notational conventions.
A displayed category over the terminal category gives precisely the data of a category.

The purpose of displayed categories is to define category-like objects that depend on other categories. We will later on learn how to impose conditions that make displayed categories behave like (potentially large) categories internal to other categories. In order to make the theory sufficiently flexible, however, it is necessary to first relativise the notion of displayed category to allow the base to be another (relative) displayed category rather than an ordinary category. The starting point of our approach to relativisation is the insight that a category is a special case of displayed category.
A displayed category over the terminal category gives precisely the data of a category.

In light of the observation above, the base of a displayed category in the sense that we have described so far is another displayed category — whose base is the terminal category. This suggests a simple way to expand our vocabulary to account for displayed categories whose base is another displayed category.

Let B\vdash C displayed precategory over a precategory B. A relative displayed precategory over C, written C\vdash E, is defined to be a displayed precategory B.C\vdash E over the total precategory B.C. A relative displayed category over C is then a displayed category over B.C.
When it is clear from context what we mean, we will speak of displayed categories over C, as in what follows, the relative version of displayed categories will be the primary one.

fr:title.text: The relative displayed precategory of slices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The relative displayed precategory of slices

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let B\vdash C be a displayed precategory; we may define a relative displayed precategory C\vdash C _{/} as follows:

fr:ol.fr:li: A displayed object b.c.p\in C _{/} is given by a vertical arrow over b\in B with codomain b.c, i.e. an arrow p\colon \partial _0 p\to c in the fiber precategory b.C.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let B\vdash C displayed precategory over a precategory B. A relative displayed precategory over C, written C\vdash E, is defined to be a displayed precategory B.C\vdash E over the total precategory B.C. A relative displayed category over C is then a displayed category over B.C.
When it is clear from context what we mean, we will speak of displayed categories over C, as in what follows, the relative version of displayed categories will be the primary one.

fr:title.text: Univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Give an example of a displayed category B\vdash C over a precategory B such that the total precategory B.C is univalent but B is nonetheless not univalent.
A displayed precategory C over a precategory B, written B\vdash C, is specified by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each b\in B, a type \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} b of displayed objects over b that we shall write b.c\in C;
for each b_0.c_0, b_1.c_1\in C and i\colon b_0\to b_1, a set C _{i} \mathopen {}\left \lparen b_0.c_0,b_1.c_1\right \rparen \mathclose {} of displayed arrows over i;
for each b.c\in C a displayed identity arrow 1_{b}.1_{c}\colon b.c\to b.c;

for each i.f\colon b_0.c_0\to b_1.c_1 and j.g\colon b_1.c_1\to b_2.c_2, a displayed composite arrow \mathopen {}\left \lparen j\circ i\right \rparen \mathclose {} \circ \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1;
such that composition is associative and unital with respect to displayed identity arrows.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A displayed precategory B\vdash C is called univalent when each of its fiber precategories b.C is univalent.
